request.question: Who owns the company that produced a CGI animated Christmas special in 1999 and has footage for its company logo taken from the short film "A Study in Wet?"

title: The Garfield Show
My Family (series 10)
The Little Troll Prince
Yogi Bear's All Star Comedy Christmas Caper
Olive, the Other Reindeer
The Curiosity Company
Grandma Got Run Over by a Reindeer (film)
Partly Cloudy
The Little Rascals Christmas Special
Descente
sentences: The Garfield Show is a French–American CGI animated television series.

Based on the American comic strip, "Garfield", the series is executive produced by Garfield creator, Jim Davis, and co-written and voice directed by Mark Evanier, who also wrote most of the episodes for the "Garfield and Friends" series.
Returning from "Garfield and Friends" are the voice actors Julie Payne (Liz) and Gregg Berger (Odie).
Frank Welker replaces Lorenzo Music (due to his death in 2001) as the voice of Garfield, and Wally Wingert replaces Thom Huge (due to his retirement that same year) as the voice of Jon Arbuckle.
Also returning is David Lander, reprising his role as Doc Boy from the earlier Garfield prime-time special "A Garfield Christmas Special" (1987).
The show is produced by Dargaud Media and Paws Inc.
The show is directed by Philippe Vidal and the music is composed by Laurent Bertaud and Jean-Christophe Prudhomme.
The tenth series of the BBC family sitcom "My Family" originally aired between 9 July 2010, and 27 August 2010, with a Christmas special that went to air on 24 December 2010.
The series was commissioned following consistent ratings from the previous series.
The opening episode, "Wheelie Ben", re-introduces the six main characters, with the addition of regular appearances from Kenzo Harper, played by Tayler Marshall.
However, the character of Roger Bailey only made an appearance in the series finale.
All episodes from the tenth series are thirty minutes in length, with the exception of the Christmas Special.
The series was once again produced by Rude Boy Productions, a company that produces comedies created by Fred Barron.
Unlike previous series of the show, which were filmed on a yearly basis, both Series 10 and 11 were filmed back-to-back.
For the first time in the show's history, two episodes of the series remained unaired for some time.
At the time of release, the DVD of the series contained two episodes that had yet to be broadcast on television.
On 17 June 2011, one of these two episodes were aired, and the other is scheduled to air on 22 July 2011.
The series averaged 4.55 million per episode; however, they managed to get over 6.00 million viewers for the Christmas Special.
The Little Troll Prince (onscreen title: The Little Troll Prince: A Christmas Parable) is a 1987 animated Christmas television special produced by Hanna-Barbera.
Backed by the International Lutheran Laymen's League, it has strong Christian themes of unconditional love, self-sacrifice, and redemption, with a substantial Protestant influence.
The story was a written parable script for the Christmas special with a lesson about the feeling of joy and love in one's heart, that can lead one from a cruel world to a light-hearted world.
Yogi Bear's All Star Comedy Christmas Caper is a 1982 animated Christmas television special starring Yogi Bear.
It is the third and final Yogi Christmas special.
Produced by Hanna-Barbera, it first aired on  21, 1982 (1982--) on CBS.
Olive, the Other Reindeer is a 1999 CGI animated Christmas television special written by Steve Young, and directed by Oscar Moore.
The feature was produced by Matt Groening's The Curiosity Company (best known for "Futurama"), and animated by DNA Productions.
It first aired on December 17, 1999 on Fox, produced by 20th Century Fox Television and Flower Films.
The special combines paperlike character art in 3-D environments.
Sometimes, traditional animation is used.
The Curiosity Company, Inc. is an American production company behind hit series "Futurama" and the 1999 television film "Olive, the Other Reindeer".
The company logo depicts the letter "C" being formed by a surfboard reflection against various ocean waves, alongside distorted water sounds.
The footage and sounds are taken from "A Study in Wet", a short film made by Matt's father, Homer.
Grandma Got Run Over by a Reindeer is a Canadian–American animated Christmas special, directed by Phil Roman.
The special first aired October 31, 2000 on The WB.
It has subsequently aired on The CW (the successor to The WB) and Cartoon Network during the Christmas season.
Since Warner Home Video distributed this special, Time Warner owns the copyrights.
The title and plot are based on the novelty song of the same name.
Partly Cloudy is a Pixar CGI animated short film written and directed by Peter Sohn and produced by Kevin Reher.
It was shown in theaters before Pixar's feature film "Up" and is a special feature on its DVD and Blu-ray release.
It was included in the Animation Show of Shows in 2009.
The Little Rascals Christmas Special is an animated Christmas television special based on the "Our Gang" comedies of the 1920s, 1930s, and early 1940s.
It first aired December 3, 1979 on NBC.

Descente Ltd. (株式会社デサント , Kabushiki-gaisha Desanto ) is a Japanese sports clothing and accessories company, first formed back in 1935, when Takeo Ishimoto started the company in Osaka as Ishimoto Shoten.
The company logo, over 50 years old, depicts 3 basic skiing techniques - traverse, schuss and side-slip.
Descente owns a portfolio of 16 in-house, purchased and licensed brands, among them Descente, Shiseist, Arena, Marmot and Srixon.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Who owns the company that produced a CGI animated Christmas special in 1999 and has footage for its company logo taken from the short film "A Study in Wet?"
hop1_docs: The Curiosity Company | The Curiosity Company, Inc. is an American production company behind hit series "Futurama" and the 1999 television film "Olive, the Other Reindeer".  The company logo depicts the letter "C" being formed by a surfboard reflection against various ocean waves, alongside distorted water sounds.  The footage and sounds are taken from "A Study in Wet", a short film made by Matt's father, Homer.

Olive, the Other Reindeer | Olive, the Other Reindeer is a 1999 CGI animated Christmas television special written by Steve Young, and directed by Oscar Moore.  The feature was produced by Matt Groening's The Curiosity Company (best known for "Futurama"), and animated by DNA Productions.  It first aired on December 17, 1999 on Fox, produced by 20th Century Fox Television and Flower Films.  The special combines paperlike character art in 3-D environments.  Sometimes, traditional animation is used.

Act3animation | Act3animation (Act3) is an Australian CGI company, specializing in character animation, based in Melbourne, Australia.  Founded in 1992 by current creative director, Mike Hollands, Act3 has produced various TVCs, FMVs and short films.

Fatkat | FatKat animation was an animation studio located in Miramichi, New Brunswick.  The company was formed from animators who left Helix Animation in 1999.  It was owned and operated by Gene Fowler and his employees.  This company was started in Halifax, Nova Scotia and was taken over by Trainingscape which saw the creation of over 40 training films for various companies.

Oxford Scientific Films | Oxford Scientific Films (OSF) is a British company that produces natural history and documentary programmes.  Founded on 8 July 1968, by noted documentary filmmaker Gerald Thompson, the independent film company broke new ground in the world of documentaries, using new filming techniques and capturing footage of never before filmed activities of its various subjects.  In 1996, Oxford Scientific Films was sold to Circle Communications, where it retained its own identity as a division within the company.  The following year, Circle Communications was taken over by Southern Star Entertainment UK.  Under the new ownership, Oxford Scientific Films continued to enhance its reputation for innovative film-making, producing multiple award-winning series and films, including the highly acclaimed Animal Planet series, "Meerkat Manor".

Sanzigen | Sanzigen Inc. (株式会社サンジゲン , Kabushiki gaisha Sanjigen ) is a Japanese animation company specializing in CGI animation.  The studio was founded in 2003, later becoming formally incorporated in 2006 by former employees of Gonzo, and has contributed to various anime series and films.  Its name is derived from the Japanese word for "three-dimensional" (三次元 , sanjigen ) .

Happy Trails Animation | Happy Trails Animation LLC, is an American animation studio specializing in feature films, commercials, music videos, broadcast graphics and short films.  It is owned by Andy and Amy Collen and is located in Portland, Oregon.  The company has two divisions, one for digital content and the other for commercial work such as advertisements and music or corporate videos.  Happy Trails Animation has received 11 Telly Awards, 1 Crystal Heart Award, 3 Davey Awards, and numerous international film festival acknowledgments from its short films over the past 18 years.
response.summary_1:
Primary topics: The Curiosity Company; Olive, the Other Reindeer; "A Study in Wet"

- The Curiosity Company: "The Curiosity Company, Inc. is an American production company" and is "behind hit series 'Futurama' and the 1999 television film 'Olive, the Other Reindeer'." (Passage 1)
- Company logo / short film: "The company logo depicts the letter 'C' ... The footage and sounds are taken from 'A Study in Wet', a short film made by Matt's father, Homer." (Passage 1)
- Olive, the Other Reindeer: "Olive, the Other Reindeer is a 1999 CGI animated Christmas television special" and was "produced by Matt Groening's The Curiosity Company" (also "animated by DNA Productions" and "first aired on December 17, 1999 on Fox"). (Passage 2)

Ambiguities / missing facts blocking direct answer:
- Missing fact: explicit/legal owner of The Curiosity Company is not stated verbatim. Bridge entity: The Curiosity Company / "Matt Groening's The Curiosity Company" (Passage 2 implies association).
- Ambiguous name: the short film maker is given only as "Matt's father, Homer" (Passage 1) — full name (e.g., Homer Groening) not stated.

Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
1) "The Curiosity Company" page — lede/infobox and "History"/"Ownership" sections (confirm registered/legal owner and founders).  
2) "Matt Groening" page — lede/infobox and "Companies" or "Career" sections (confirm his ownership/founding of The Curiosity Company).  
3) "A Study in Wet" page or "Homer Groening" page — lede/credits (confirm author/director name and that footage/sounds match the logo source).  
4) "Olive, the Other Reindeer" page — infobox/production section (confirm producer credit lines and production companies).
response.hop2_query: The Curiosity Company owner Matt Groening
hop2_docs: Olive, the Other Reindeer | Olive, the Other Reindeer is a 1999 CGI animated Christmas television special written by Steve Young, and directed by Oscar Moore.  The feature was produced by Matt Groening's The Curiosity Company (best known for "Futurama"), and animated by DNA Productions.  It first aired on December 17, 1999 on Fox, produced by 20th Century Fox Television and Flower Films.  The special combines paperlike character art in 3-D environments.  Sometimes, traditional animation is used.
List of awards and nominations received by Matt Groening | List of awards and nominations received by Matt Groening

The Curiosity Company | The Curiosity Company, Inc. is an American production company behind hit series "Futurama" and the 1999 television film "Olive, the Other Reindeer".  The company logo depicts the letter "C" being formed by a surfboard reflection against various ocean waves, alongside distorted water sounds.  The footage and sounds are taken from "A Study in Wet", a short film made by Matt's father, Homer.

List of recurring Futurama characters | "Futurama" is an American animated science fiction sitcom created by Matt Groening and developed by Groening and David X. Cohen for the Fox Broadcasting Company.  The series follows the adventures of a late-20th-century New York City pizza delivery boy, Philip J. Fry, who, after being unwittingly cryogenically frozen for one thousand years, finds employment at Planet Express, an interplanetary delivery company in the retro-futuristic 31st century.

Bongo Comics Group | Bongo Comics Group is a comic book publishing company founded in 1993 by Matt Groening along with Steve & Cindy Vance and Bill Morrison.  It publishes comics related to the animated television series "The Simpsons" and "Futurama", as well as the "SpongeBob SquarePants" comic; along with original material.  It was named after Bongo, a rabbit character in Groening's comic strip "Life in Hell".

Futurama | Futurama is an American animated science fiction comedy series created by Matt Groening for the Fox Broadcasting Company.  The series follows the adventures of a late-20th-century New York City pizza delivery boy, Philip J. Fry, who, after being unwittingly cryogenically frozen for one thousand years, finds employment at Planet Express, an interplanetary delivery company in the retro-futuristic 31st century.  The series was envisioned by Groening in the mid-1990s while working on "The Simpsons"; he later brought David X. Cohen aboard to develop storylines and characters to pitch the show to Fox.

Skyworks Global | Skyworks Global, formerly Groen Aeronautics Corporation and then Groen Brothers Aviation, Inc., is an American autogyro research and development company based in Salt Lake City, Utah.  The company was founded in 1986 by David Groen and his late brother Jay Groen.  David Groen remains as Senior Advisor.
